prequiz.title: How HTTPS Works
prequiz.subtitle: Key terms before you start
prequiz.description: Review these concepts before tackling the HTTPS quiz. Understanding them will help you answer questions about secure web communication, TLS handshakes, and certificates.

keywords.definition: HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure — HTTP with an added TLS encryption layer. Runs on port 443 by default.
The modern cryptographic protocol that provides privacy and data integrity between two communicating applications. Replaced SSL.
The predecessor to TLS. All SSL versions are now deprecated due to security flaws. The term is still loosely used but TLS is what's actually used today.
The negotiation process at the start of a TLS connection where the client and server agree on a protocol version, cipher suite, and establish a shared session key.
A trusted organization that issues and digitally signs TLS certificates, verifying domain or organization identity. Examples: Let's Encrypt, DigiCert.
A digital document that binds a public key to a domain name. Signed by a CA so that browsers can verify the server's identity.
A key pair used in asymmetric cryptography. The public key is shared openly; the private key is kept secret on the server. Data encrypted with one can only be decrypted with the other.
Encryption using a key pair (public + private). Used during TLS handshake for key exchange. Slower than symmetric, but enables secure communication without pre-shared keys.
Encryption using a single shared secret key for both encryption and decryption. Used for bulk data transfer in TLS because it is much faster than asymmetric encryption.

A named combination of algorithms specifying how key exchange, authentication, bulk encryption, and message authentication are performed in a TLS session (e.g., TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384).
A hierarchy of certificates linking the server's certificate through one or more intermediate CAs back to a trusted root CA stored in the browser/OS.
The top-level Certificate Authority whose certificate is self-signed and pre-installed in browsers and operating systems as a trust anchor.
A key-exchange property (achieved with ECDHE or DHE) ensuring that if a server's private key is later compromised, past recorded sessions cannot be decrypted.
A browser security policy sent via the `Strict-Transport-Security` HTTP header, instructing the browser to always use HTTPS for the domain and reject plain HTTP connections.
A TLS extension where the client sends the target hostname at the start of the handshake, allowing a single server IP to serve multiple TLS certificates (virtual hosting).
A protocol for real-time certificate revocation checking. The browser queries the CA to see if a certificate has been revoked before trusting it.
A periodically published list of certificates revoked by a CA before their expiry date. Browsers can download it to check certificate validity.
An attack where a third party intercepts communication between client and server. HTTPS certificate validation prevents MitM by ensuring the server's identity is verified.
The most basic TLS certificate type, confirming only that the applicant controls the domain. Issued automatically (e.g., by Let's Encrypt) without verifying organization identity.
The latest TLS version (2018). Removed weak algorithms, reduced handshake to 1-RTT, and added 0-RTT resumption for faster reconnections while maintaining security.
